summary: High‑energy ’89 Shoreline set with killer Pocky Way jam and moving He’s Gone
blurb: The show delivers strong transitions like Foolish Heart → Jack Straw and a scorching Hey Pocky Way → Iko Iko sequence. Jerry shines on He's Gone, while Brent supplies some of his best B‑3 work of the era. Overall performance is energetic with a few uneven spots.
review: June 18, 1989 at Shoreline Amphitheatre captured a restless, high‑octane Dead show that still feels fresh today. The first set rolls off with tight segues – Foolish Heart into Jack Straw and a wild Alabama Getaway – before diving into the standout Hey Pocky Way → Iko Iko jam where Jerry’s lead guitar is described as “killer” and Brent’s organ solo hot enough to be called “the Jerry Garcia of the keyboards.” The second set builds momentum with a heartfelt He's Gone that many reviewers called mournful and moving, followed by a driving Wheel intro and a solid run through classics like Cumberland Blues and Cold Rain And Snow. While moments such as Saint Of Circumstance felt sloppy and Samson & Delilah started unevenly, the band’s overall groove stayed on its feet, making this an engaging snapshot of the late‑’80s era. The recommended soundboard (gd1989-06-18.sbd.walker-scotton.miller.83635.sbeok.flac16) offers crisp, well‑balanced audio that showcases these highs without drowning out the occasional thin bass or Brent’s erratic bursts.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Multiple recordings praise key moments (Hey Pocky Way → IKO jam, He's Gone, Foolish Heart) and note high energy, while others point out sloppy spots (Saint Of Circumstance, Samson). The overall tone is mildly positive with more positives than negatives, yielding a modest +0.8 sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Crisp, well‑balanced soundboard capture that highlights the show’s transitions and solos while minimizing technical flaws.

key_highlights: Killer Hey Pocky Way → Iko Iko jam with scorching guitar and hot keyboards
Emotional He's Gone performance that stands out as a centerpiece of the second set
Smooth segues from Foolish Heart into Jack Straw and a driving Wheel intro
Brent’s B‑3 solo work praised as some of his best, despite occasional erratic moments

band_performance.Jerry: Delivers a heartfelt phrasing on He's Gone and a killer lead on Hey Pocky Way, with heavy‑fanning solos that drive the high‑energy jams; occasional puzzled look during Brent’s solo hints at the set's rawness.
band_performance.Phil: Bass generally solid but noted as a bit thin in one mix; otherwise no major performance issues reported.
band_performance.Brent: Provides some of his most praised B‑3 work and a scorching organ solo on Hey Pocky Way, though moments of erratic or ‘wasted’ playing and a brief fall off the stool are mentioned, giving an overall energetic but sometimes uneven keyboard presence.
